results.possibleIdentity: Ben-Ami Kadish (1923–2012) was an American mechanical and production engineer who worked for the U.S. Army at the Picatinny Arsenal in Dover, New Jersey, from 1963 to 1990. During the late 1960s and 1970s, he resided in Clifton, New Jersey, where he was a prominent member of the local community, serving as the president of the Men's Club at the Clifton Jewish Center. Professionally, he was a supervisory engineer in the Fuze Division of the Army Armament Research, Development and Engineering Center. In 1968, he participated in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, collaborating with artist Giselle Mauer on a project titled 'Mauer & Kadish' for the exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' held at the Brooklyn Museum. In 2008, Kadish gained international attention when he was arrested and subsequently pleaded guilty to acting as an unregistered agent for the government of Israel. He admitted to providing classified documents—including information on nuclear weapons, the Patriot missile system, and the F-15 fighter jet—to an Israeli handler between 1979 and 1985, a case that was linked to the Jonathan Pollard espionage scandal.
